Human voice vs committee 4/10 — Mostly committee-voice boilerplate, with occasional first-person flashes that feel bolted on.
Genuine opinions vs safe claims 6/10 — A few sharp takes (E-E-A-T is theater, most AEO advice is garbage) exist but are surrounded by hedged, generic claims.
Unexpected phrasings 5/10 — Two or three memorable lines, but most prose is tri-colon AI cadence ("structuring, clarifying, and validating").
Specific citations 4/10 — Names platforms but gives no prices, dates, percentages, study results, or people—just bracketed placeholder refs.
Point of view vs survey 2/10 — Takes no position; hedges every assertion with safe qualifiers.
Quotable sentence 1/10 — Nothing memorable; every sentence is interchangeable generic SaaS blog prose.
Intro earns attention 2/10 — Opens with a dictionary definition, the most predictable possible hook.

specificity 5/10 — The post names tools and schema types well, but several sentences float in abstractions — 'authority,' 'visibility,' 'relevance,' 'signals' — without attaching numbers, named case studies, or concrete benchmarks. Phrases like 'organizations can ensure continuous brand relevance' and 'gain citation share, authority, and visibility' are vague assertions. The post cites Semrush and arXiv papers in brackets but never quotes a single specific finding, statistic, or example from them, which undermines the specificity it promises.
